When the wall was nearly complete—still missing only its gates—Nehemiah's enemies made their move. They could not stop the work with open opposition, so Sanballat and his allies switched tactics. Four times they invited Nehemiah to meet them on neutral ground, their words seemingly innocent. Four times he refused, offering the same steady reply: the work is too important. But on the fifth attempt, they escalated. An unsealed letter arrived, carrying gossip designed to reach the king's ears—false accusations that Nehemiah was building the wall as a prelude to rebellion, that he had appointed prophets to declare him king. The lie was crafted to sound plausible, whispered by "all the nations," laced with just enough specificity to sting. Yet Nehemiah's response was neither defensive nor wavering. He simply stated the truth: "There is nothing to these rumors you are spreading; you are inventing them in your own mind." Then he prayed a single sentence that captures his entire posture: "My God, strengthen my hands."

The pressure did not end. Shemaiah, a man confined to his house, approached Nehemiah with what sounded like an urgent prophecy. They were coming to kill him at night, Shemaiah warned. They must meet inside the temple itself, shut the doors, hide. The advice sounded reasonable. A man of faith would trust a warning about danger, would seek safety in God's house. But Nehemiah refused. "Should a man like me run away? Should one like me go into the temple to save his own life? I will not go!" These are not the words of a reckless fool but of a man who understands something crucial: abandoning his post would be sin, regardless of how it was dressed up. Nehemiah recognized what was actually happening. Shemaiah had been hired. The enemies wanted him to hide in the temple—not to save his life, but to discredit him, to make him look like a coward or a false leader. They wanted him to sin so they could have grounds to attack his character.

What makes this moment devastating is not the external opposition but the corruption within. As the wall neared completion, Nehemiah discovered that many nobles of Judah were bound by oath to Tobiah. Family connections ran deep—Tobiah's relatives had married into prominent Judean families. Letters passed back and forth. Reports of Tobiah's "good deeds" reached Nehemiah's ears while Tobiah simultaneously sent letters designed to intimidate. The battle for Jerusalem was not only against external enemies but against a people divided, against relationships compromised by worldly entanglement. Yet despite this internal fracture, despite the lies, the hired prophets, the family loyalties pulling in different directions, the wall was completed. Fifty-two days. Twenty-fifth of Elul. The work was done. And when the surrounding nations learned of it, they were afraid, "for they realized that this task had been accomplished by our God." This is the reality that sustained Nehemiah through every threat: God had done this work. The enemies' schemes, the intimidation, the lies, the internal corruption—none of it could stop what God had determined to do.
